place.name: Kingston upon Hull
place.summary: An estuary city shaped by glacial landscapes, medieval port foundations, Civil War confrontation, abolition, docks, fishing, wartime destruction, engineering ambition and modern cultural reinvention.

entries.title: Glacial Processes Shape the Humber Estuary Landscape
entries.description: During the last Ice Age, glaciers, meltwater and later estuarine processes helped shape the low-lying Humber basin. As ice retreated, river systems and tidal forces created the broad estuary and flat alluvial ground that would later make Hull possible.
entries.significance: Hull’s existence depends on the deep-time creation of the Humber estuary and its surrounding lowlands.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the Humber estuary and lower River Hull landscape around the historic city.

entries.title: Postglacial Silts and Marshlands Build the Ground Beneath Hull
entries.description: After the retreat of the ice, tidal deposition and river-borne sediments built up the silts, mudflats and marshy terrain around the Humber and lower River Hull. These soft postglacial deposits created the flat but flood-prone landscape on which the later town was established.
entries.significance: The ground beneath Hull is itself a historical actor: fertile, low-lying, strategic, and always in negotiation with water.
entries.area_note: Associated with the alluvial lowlands of the old town, docks and river mouth.

entries.title: The River Hull Meets the Humber and Creates a Natural Port Landscape
entries.description: The meeting of the River Hull and the Humber estuary created a natural point of exchange between inland Yorkshire and maritime routes to the North Sea and beyond. This confluence gave the area unusual strategic and commercial value long before the city itself existed.
entries.significance: Hull’s future as a port city begins with geography: river meeting estuary in exactly the right place.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the confluence zone around the old harbour and lower River Hull.

entries.title: Wyke upon Hull Emerges as a River Trading Settlement
entries.description: Before it became Kingston upon Hull, the area was known as Wyke upon Hull, a small settlement positioned to benefit from river and estuary trade. Its location made it a useful point of movement between inland Yorkshire and maritime shipping lanes.
entries.significance: This early settlement was the seed from which the royal port city of Hull would grow.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the old town and river-port core from which medieval Hull developed.

entries.title: Edward I Purchases Wyke and Founds Kingston upon Hull
entries.description: In 1299 Edward I acquired the settlement of Wyke and re-founded it as Kingston upon Hull. Its location at the junction of the River Hull and the Humber made it an ideal royal port for trade, military supply and administration.
entries.significance: This act formally created Hull as a royal town and set the course for its future as one of England’s great ports.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the medieval old town and royal port core.

entries.title: Hull Grows into a Major Medieval Wool Port
entries.description: In the later Middle Ages Hull became one of England’s major ports, especially for the export of wool and cloth and the import of goods from northern Europe. Its prosperity drove urban growth, civic institutions and the increasing fortification of the town.
entries.significance: Hull’s medieval commercial success established the maritime identity that would define the city for centuries.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the old harbour and merchant district of the old town.

entries.title: Hull’s Walls and Beverley Gate Reinforce the Medieval Town
entries.description: As Hull grew in strategic and commercial importance, the town was enclosed with walls, ditches and gates. Beverley Gate became one of the most important entrances to the fortified town and later played a famous role in the Civil War.
entries.significance: Hull was not just a port but a defended urban stronghold whose physical boundaries shaped later political events.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the west side of the old town around the site of Beverley Gate and town defences.

entries.title: Hull Refuses Entry to Charles I
entries.description: In April 1642 Sir John Hotham refused King Charles I entry to Hull, denying him access to the large arsenal stored there. The confrontation was one of the first major acts of defiance leading directly into the English Civil War.
entries.significance: Hull became one of the first great flashpoints of the Civil War and briefly stood at the hinge of national history.
entries.area_note: Traditionally associated with Beverley Gate and the town defences.

entries.title: Hull Endures the First Siege of the English Civil War
entries.description: Later in 1642 Hull was besieged by Royalist forces seeking to capture the town and its military stores. The town held out, helping establish its reputation as a crucial Parliamentarian stronghold.
entries.significance: The siege confirmed Hull’s strategic value and reinforced its role in the opening phase of the Civil War.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the fortified town and defensive perimeter of early modern Hull.

entries.title: Hull Resists a Second Civil War Siege
entries.description: A second major siege in 1643 again tested Hull’s defences. The town remained in Parliamentarian hands, preserving a vital northern stronghold and supply base.
entries.significance: Hull’s survival as a defended port had consequences far beyond the city itself, shaping the wider military balance in the north.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the defensive circuit and old town core.

entries.title: William Wilberforce Born in Hull
entries.description: William Wilberforce, the leading parliamentary campaigner against the British slave trade, was born in Hull in 1759. His later career made the city inseparable from one of the most consequential moral and political struggles in British history.
entries.significance: Hull became permanently linked to the abolition movement through one of its most famous sons.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the Wilberforce House area in the old town.

entries.title: Hull Opens the Old Dock, a Landmark in Dock Engineering
entries.description: Hull’s Old Dock opened in 1778 and is often regarded as the first commercial wet dock in Britain. It transformed the practical handling of shipping and marked the city as an innovator in port infrastructure.
entries.significance: This was a major moment in the engineering history of British docks and strengthened Hull’s role as a leading maritime city.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the site later occupied by Queen’s Gardens, formerly the Old Dock.

entries.title: Hull Becomes a Major Whaling Port
entries.description: In the late eighteenth and nineteenth centuries Hull became a major British whaling port, sending ships to Arctic waters and bringing whale oil and bone back through the Humber. The trade tied the city to global maritime networks and dangerous northern voyages.
entries.significance: Hull’s prosperity and maritime culture were shaped not only by local trade but by long-distance extractive industries in the Arctic.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the dockside and wharf areas of the expanding port.

entries.title: Albert Dock Opens as Hull’s Dock System Expands
entries.description: The opening of Albert Dock in 1869 formed part of Hull’s enormous nineteenth-century dock expansion. New basins, rail links and warehouses reinforced the city’s status as one of Britain’s major maritime and commercial hubs.
entries.significance: Hull’s dock network turned the city into an industrial maritime powerhouse.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the east docklands and Humber waterfront.

entries.title: Hull Becomes One of the World’s Great Fishing Ports
entries.description: From the late nineteenth into the mid twentieth century, Hull developed into one of the world’s major fishing ports. Trawlers, fish docks, markets and processing industries shaped the labour, soundscape and daily identity of the city.
entries.significance: Fishing was not just an industry in Hull but a defining way of life.
entries.area_note: Anchored to St Andrew’s Dock, fish dock infrastructure and the western docklands.

entries.title: Amy Johnson Born in Hull
entries.description: Pioneering aviator Amy Johnson was born in Hull in 1903. Her achievements in long-distance solo flight made her one of the most famous women of her era and a lasting part of Hull’s civic identity.
entries.significance: Amy Johnson gave Hull a heroic modern figure associated with courage, innovation and flight.
entries.area_note: Anchored to central Hull as part of the city’s wider civic memory.

entries.title: Hull Suffers Devastating Bombing During the Second World War
entries.description: Hull was one of the most heavily bombed cities in Britain during the Second World War because of its docks, industry and strategic position. Vast areas of housing, warehouses and civic fabric were damaged or destroyed in repeated air raids.
entries.significance: The Blitz reshaped the physical city and left deep scars in Hull’s twentieth-century memory.
entries.area_note: Anchored broadly to the old town, docklands and city centre heavily affected by bombing.

entries.title: The Triple Trawler Disaster Shocks Hull
entries.description: In early 1968 Hull was devastated by the loss of three trawlers in rapid succession, with many crewmen killed. The tragedy exposed the dangers faced by fishing communities and triggered public outrage.
entries.significance: The disaster became one of the defining modern tragedies of Hull’s maritime history.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the fishing docklands and communities tied to the trawler fleet.

entries.title: The Headscarf Revolution Forces Fishing Safety Reform
entries.description: After the Triple Trawler Disaster, campaigners led by Hull women including Lillian Bilocca pushed for major improvements in safety for trawlermen. Their activism became known as the Headscarf Revolution and forced change in the fishing industry.
entries.significance: Hull’s maritime history was reshaped not only by tragedy but by fierce local activism and working-class political pressure.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the fishing communities and dockland neighbourhoods of west Hull.

entries.title: The Humber Bridge Opens
entries.description: When it opened in 1981, the Humber Bridge had the longest single-span suspension bridge in the world. Though west of the old town, it became one of the defining structures of greater Hull and the Humber region.
entries.significance: The bridge symbolised engineering ambition and permanently changed how the estuary was experienced and crossed.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the Humber Bridge as part of the wider Hull and Humber city-region story.

entries.title: The Deep Opens and Recasts Hull’s Waterfront Identity
entries.description: The Deep opened in 2002 at Sammy’s Point near the meeting of the River Hull and the Humber. Combining aquarium, research, education and landmark architecture, it became one of the most recognisable symbols of modern Hull.
entries.significance: The Deep turned the city’s waterfront into a site of scientific interpretation and civic rebranding.
entries.area_note: Directly anchored to The Deep and the river-estuary edge of the old port.

entries.title: Hull Becomes UK City of Culture
entries.description: In 2017 Hull became UK City of Culture, hosting major installations, performances and public events across the city. The year helped transform national perceptions of Hull and highlighted its creative and civic energy.
entries.significance: This marked a major modern reintroduction of Hull to itself and to the wider country.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the city centre and old town where many City of Culture events were staged.

metadata.notes: Hull Super Pack. Designed as a flagship multi-layer urban-estuary pack spanning geology, royal foundation, Civil War, abolition, docks, fishing, wartime destruction, activism, engineering and cultural renewal.
